content: What is the electromagnetic spectrum?
The electromagnetic spectrum is the range of all possible frequencies of electromagnetic radiation, from very low-frequency radio waves to very high-frequency gamma rays.
What are the main regions?
The main regions, from low to high frequency: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays.
Where does visible light fit?
Visible light occupies a narrow band roughly 380-750 nanometers in wavelength, corresponding to frequencies around 400-790 terahertz. It's a tiny slice of the full spectrum.
What about radio waves?
Radio waves have the lowest frequencies and longest wavelengths in the spectrum, ranging from millimeters to thousands of kilometers. They're used for broadcasting, communications, and radar.
And gamma rays?
Gamma rays have the highest frequencies and shortest wavelengths, produced by radioactive decay and high-energy astrophysical processes. They're ionizing and can be dangerous to biological tissue.
Now I want to dive deeper into EM waves. What actually IS an electromagnetic wave physically?

An electromagnetic wave is a self-propagating disturbance in coupled electric and magnetic fields. A changing electric field induces a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field induces an electric field. The two fields oscillate perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation, creating a transverse wave that propagates at the speed of light without needing any medium.
How did Maxwell predict this?

In the 1860s, James Clerk Maxwell unified four equations describing electricity and magnetism (now Maxwell's equations). When combined, they predicted wave solutions propagating at speed c = 1/sqrt(ε₀μ₀), where ε₀ and μ₀ are the electric and magnetic constants of vacuum. This value matched the experimentally measured speed of light — strong evidence that light IS an electromagnetic wave.
What does polarization tell us about EM waves?

Polarization describes the orientation of the electric field oscillation in an EM wave. Light from most sources is unpolarized (random orientations), but it can be polarized by reflection, scattering, or polarizing filters. Linear polarization has the field oscillating in one plane; circular polarization has the field rotating. Polarization is direct evidence that EM waves are transverse, not longitudinal.
How does the wave nature explain interference and diffraction?

When EM waves from coherent sources overlap, they add or cancel based on phase relationships — constructive interference where peaks align, destructive where peak meets trough. This produces interference patterns. Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles or through apertures, characteristic of all wave phenomena. Both were demonstrated for light (Young's double-slit, 1801) before EM theory existed.
What's the relationship between energy and frequency?

For an electromagnetic wave treated quantum mechanically, energy comes in discrete photons with E = hf, where h is Planck's constant and f is frequency. So higher-frequency radiation (X-rays, gamma rays) carries more energy per photon than lower-frequency radiation (radio, microwaves). This explains why UV and higher can ionize atoms while radio cannot.
How does this connect to the spectrum we discussed earlier?

Each region of the electromagnetic spectrum is the same fundamental phenomenon (oscillating EM fields) differentiated only by frequency/wavelength. The differences in behavior — radio passing through walls, X-rays passing through tissue, gamma rays ionizing matter — all stem from how photons of different energies interact with matter at the atomic and nuclear scale.
